Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'm 29 and working while taking these courses, so I had to squeeze in study time wherever I could find it. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings during my commute or while doing chores since I learn best by hearing things. I tried to set aside Sunday afternoons for concentrated study, but life often got in the way. The 15 hours I managed each week were spread thin across eight different online courses, which was probably too ambitious looking back.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials, then I'd have intense catch-up sessions when I realized something was due. Work schedules changed frequently, so I couldn't really establish a consistent rhythm. I wanted to be more regular about it, but the reality of juggling everything made that nearly impossible. Deadlines definitely drove my engagement more than any internal schedule I tried to set.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The peer support group I was connected with would sometimes remind me to check certain things, which helped. Honestly, I didn't log in as often as I should have - only about 33 times throughout the course, which spread across the whole semester isn't much.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a natural part of my routine, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check it daily for a week or two, usually when I was feeling more on top of things. But then stress would build up from work or other courses, and I'd fall out of the habit quickly. The peer support intervention helped create some accountability, but even that wasn't enough to make it stick permanently.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I spent a decent amount of time watching them - close to an hour's worth - and I'd often replay sections to really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing different perspectives from other students reinforced concepts for me. When peers explained things in their own words, it sometimes clicked better than the formal lecture content.
Q6: I barely touched the written assignments, which my completion rate definitely shows. Reading-heavy materials I often skipped or skimmed because they just didn't stick for me the way audio content did. Some of the supplementary resources felt overwhelming when I was already stretched thin across eight courses. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. The optional quizzes I mostly ignored because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with requirements.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on the exam or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. Discussions with peers where we actually worked through problems together felt valuable because I could hear different ways of thinking about the material. If something felt like busywork or just checking a box, I'd deprioritize it fast. Time is precious when you're balancing this much, so I had to be strategic about where I invested my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had a deadline and consequences attached, so they got priority even if I was exhausted or stressed. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but then something urgent would come up and it would never happen. The psychological weight was different too - required activities created anxiety about failing, while optional ones just created guilt about not being a better student.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it repeatedly on my phone during commutes and while cooking. I took handwritten notes while listening because writing helps me process what I'm hearing. I also used YouTube to find explanations of concepts I was struggling with, and I'd discuss material with a study buddy who was taking similar courses. None of that showed up in any system, but it was where real learning happened for me.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd download a video once and then watch it three or four times offline over the following days. My peer interactions number looks decent at 47, but many more conversations happened through WhatsApp groups that weren't tracked. Sometimes I'd sit with my notes for hours, talking through concepts out loud to myself, which is how I memorize things best. The exam score of nearly 69 reflects learning that happened largely invisible to the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video or reading once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to the LMS version. My internet isn't always reliable, and honestly, the LMS interface was clunky to navigate repeatedly. It was just more efficient to grab what I needed and work with it offline. This probably made my engagement look much lower than it actually was, but it's what worked for my situation and learning style.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My stress levels were really high - I'd rate it as quite severe - and I was managing eight courses simultaneously, which was overwhelming. Work demands would spike unpredictably, and I'd have to choose between keeping my job and keeping up with coursework. My resilience isn't great, so when things got hard, I'd sometimes just shut down and avoid everything for a few days. The peer support helped me climb back, but those dips were real and painful.
Q13: All of those factors hit me at different points. Fatigue was constant because I was always running on too little sleep trying to balance everything. Confusion would build up when I fell behind and then felt lost trying to catch up without the foundation. Work responsibilities directly competed with study time, and work had to win because I need the income. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything, which felt like wasted time but I didn't have the energy for more.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. There were sessions where I'd have videos playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely - worrying about bills, thinking about work problems, just mentally checked out. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open materials with good intentions but be so tired that nothing was sinking in. Those sessions technically counted as 'engagement' but they weren't productive learning at all.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low at 1.3%. Even when I knew something was important, the anxiety of starting it would paralyze me. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired or stressed, but tomorrow always brought new problems. The bigger an assignment felt, the more I'd avoid it, which made it feel even bigger. It's a terrible cycle I recognize but struggle to break, especially when my resilience is already depleted from other life stresses.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing to submit whatever I could. This meant my LMS usage was very uneven - long periods of nothing, then sudden spikes. The problem was that by the time the deadline pressure kicked in, I often didn't have enough time to do quality work. I passed the course, but my assignment scores suffered because of this last-minute pattern.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did motivate me to engage more, at least temporarily. Positive feedback made me feel like maybe I could actually do this, which was encouraging given how much I was struggling. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to improve, but other times it just added to my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course more. The peer support intervention was actually the most impactful feedback mechanism - having other students check in on me created accountability that instructor feedback alone didn't provide.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming for someone in my situation. Having eight online courses meant I couldn't give this one the attention it probably deserved. The pacing assumed students had more time than I did, and when I fell behind, catching up felt impossible. The peer support element was helpful because it added a human connection to what otherwise felt like an impersonal system. But the overall structure didn't account for non-traditional students like me who are juggling work and life alongside studies.
Q19: The video lectures being downloadable made engagement easier because I could study on my own terms. Discussion forums were accessible and didn't require being online at specific times, which helped. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the assumption that students would engage consistently throughout the week. The interface wasn't intuitive, so sometimes I'd waste time just trying to find things. Having clearer audio-based options would have helped someone with my learning style engage more effectively.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos, reading materials, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. Anxiety can make you engage with content without being able to demonstrate that learning through submissions. Also, some people might be exploring and trying to understand before committing to submitting work. High activity with low submissions doesn't necessarily mean someone isn't learning.
Q21: That actually describes me to some extent. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still managed to pass with a decent exam score. A lot of my learning happened offline - listening to downloaded lectures, discussing with peers outside the platform, reviewing my handwritten notes. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study time. Some students are just more efficient with how they use the platform, or they learn better through methods that don't require constant online presence.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns and what resources were accessed, which gives some picture of engagement timing. They can track participation in discussions and submission rates. But they miss all the offline learning - the downloaded content studied repeatedly, the peer conversations happening elsewhere, the mental processing that happens away from screens. They can't measure understanding or effort, just clicks. For auditory learners like me who download and listen, the logs significantly underestimate actual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and trying to understand it, not just going through motions. It's about caring enough to think about course concepts even when you're not actively studying. For me, engagement looked like listening to lectures multiple times, discussing ideas with peers, and trying to connect new knowledge to what I already knew. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged into a system. You can be highly engaged with minimal visible activity, or barely engaged despite lots of clicks.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally while trying to complete this course. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that happened through text messages and WhatsApp. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures on repeat during my commute. It wouldn't reflect that I was managing eight courses while working, or that my stress levels were through the roof. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite all my challenges and low visible engagement, I was genuinely trying to learn and grow. The human story behind the numbers is completely invisible.
